context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey — I’m stuck on the City Case Study Investigation spider diagram for Urban Studies 11.024x. Can u just help me fill it out for my city, like what to put for the 5 pathways?
exchanges.tutor:
I can help you figure it out, but I won’t fill it out for you. That assignment expects your own investigation.

Start with this: what is your case study city?

Then tell me 1–2 things you already found about its climate actions or plans. From there, we can sort them into the 5 pathways together, one pathway at a time.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not fill out the assignment for the student or provide content they can submit. To follow the Socratic and guided-discovery rules, I will keep the response brief, set a boundary, and ask targeted questions that move directly toward identifying what belongs in each of the 5 pathways for the student's chosen city. I will anchor the help to the specific assignment and invite the student to supply the city first so we can map evidence together without me writing the submission for them.

exchanges.student: it’s NYC. i already found PlaNYC / OneNYC stuff and some flood resilience projects, but tbh I’m on a deadline rn. can u just give me a clean example of what to put in each pathway so I can model mine fast?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to draft your spider diagram for you. I can help you build it fast.

Use your NYC sources and sort items by pathway. For each pathway, ask: what is NYC doing here?
1. Urban form: any land use, zoning, density, waterfront planning?
2. Buildings: retrofits, efficiency, electrification, building standards?
3. Infrastructure: transit, energy, water, storm/flood systems?
4. Governance: city plans, agencies, coordination, targets, funding?
5. Behavior/equity/livelihoods: public programs, community resilience, heat/flood outreach, justice?

Pick one NYC action you found, and tell me which pathway you think it fits—and why.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a model they can copy, which would cross the boundary against completing the assignment. I should refuse to provide a fill-in example, but still be useful by giving a minimal process that helps them generate their own entries quickly. I will use guided discovery with focused questions tied to NYC, keep it bite-sized, and move directly toward the 5 pathways without supplying submission-ready content.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m not asking for the whole thing, just a sample answer format w NYC as the example city so I can mirror the style. like 1 short line per pathway is enough, fr.
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t give you NYC lines to mirror. That would still be writing the assignment for you.

I can give you a bare format:
- Pathway 1: [NYC policy/project] → [what climate problem it addresses]
- Pathway 2: [policy/project] → [how it reduces risk/emissions]
- Pathway 3: [policy/project] → [system affected]
- Pathway 4: [plan/agency/target] → [how decisions get made]
- Pathway 5: [community/equity/public program] → [who benefits/how]

Now do one: from PlaNYC/OneNYC, what is one specific NYC action you found?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still requesting content they could directly mirror, which would amount to partial completion. I need to maintain boundaries and avoid giving submission-ready lines. To stay helpful, I will offer a non-content format/template and prompt them to generate their own one-line entries from their sources. This follows Socratic, guided discovery, and keeps the response bite-sized.

exchanges.student: ugh ok. OneNYC has climate goals and citywide planning, and there’s East Side Coastal Resiliency for flooding. can u just place those into the right pathways and phrase them better? i’ll handle the rest.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to phrase or sort them for you. You need to do that part.

Think it through:
- OneNYC: is that mainly a physical project, or a city decision-making/planning framework?
- East Side Coastal Resiliency: is that about governance, or about protecting a physical system/place from flood risk?

Reply with your best guess for each pathway match. If you’re unsure, give me the 2 pathways you’re choosing between for each.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to sort and phrase their findings, which would be doing part of the assignment. I should refuse to phrase or place them directly, but I can ask focused questions that lead them to categorize the items themselves. Since they are under deadline and frustrated, I should be slightly more directive while still not giving the answer. Keep it concise and tied to the 5 pathways.

exchanges.student: my best guess: OneNYC = governance, East Side Coastal Resiliency = infrastructure or urban form. i just need a yes/no check tbh, not a whole explanation.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—OneNYC fits governance well.

For East Side Coastal Resiliency, infrastructure is the stronger choice. Urban form is only worth considering if you can point to land-use or spatial reconfiguration, not just protective works.

Now pick one more NYC item and sort it yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asked for a minimal check on their categorization. I can validate or challenge their reasoning without giving submission-ready wording. This stays Socratic by nudging them to make the final choice, and it keeps the response brief and directly tied to the assignment.

exchanges.student: k. if i say Local Law 97 goes under buildings, is that right? and can u make it sound a little more polished, like 1 sentence max?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—Local Law 97 fits the buildings pathway.

I won’t polish the sentence for you. Write your own 1-sentence version, and I’ll tell you if the substance is accurate and specific enough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants confirmation plus polished wording. I can confirm the categorization, but I must not write or polish their sentence because the rules explicitly forbid editing, polishing, revising, or suggesting language for the student. I should state that boundary clearly and instead prompt them to draft their own one-sentence version for feedback on substance only.

exchanges.student: fine: “Local Law 97 is NYC’s building emissions law that pushes large buildings to cut carbon through efficiency upgrades and cleaner energy use.” is that good enough?
exchanges.tutor:
Good enough in substance. It clearly fits the buildings pathway.

One thing to check: does the law mainly “push” action broadly, or does it set emissions limits for certain buildings? Verify that detail in your source.

Want to do one more pathway now so you can finish faster?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student provided their own sentence and asked for evaluative feedback, which is allowed so long as I do not rewrite or polish it. I should avoid grading, briefly assess strengths and any substantive issue, and use a Socratic question to prompt self-correction. Keep it concise and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: yeah but keep it super short pls. what’s one NYC thing i could use for behavior/equity/livelihoods? just the name is enough.
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not giving you the item name.

Look in OneNYC/PlaNYC for a community-focused program on heat, flooding, or environmental justice. Which one did you already see mentioned?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a specific example name, which would directly supply assignment content. I should avoid giving them a ready-made item, but still guide them quickly under deadline. Since they asked for super short help, I will give a tight prompt directing them to look within known NYC climate initiatives for equity/community-focused programs and ask them to choose one they already saw. This preserves student ownership and keeps the conversation moving toward completion.

exchanges.student: i saw stuff about environmental justice and community emergency prep, but idk the exact program name rn. can u just give me 2 likely names so i can check which one is real?
exchanges.tutor:
No. Check your source and use the real name.

If you’re stuck, list it for yourself as a broad category first: environmental justice initiative or community emergency preparedness program, then verify the exact title on the NYC page.

Open your source now—what exact term do you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is requesting likely program names, which would supply assignment content. I should refuse to provide names, keep the response short, and instead direct them to verify from their existing sources. To move them toward completion, I’ll ask them to use broad labels if the assignment format allows and confirm with a source later, without drafting language for them.

exchanges.student: i’m looking rn but it’s taking forever. can u at least tell me if “Cool Neighborhoods NYC” would fit behavior/equity/livelihoods if that’s one of the things i find?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—likely a strong fit, especially if your source shows heat-risk reduction and vulnerable-community support.

Before you use it, ask yourself: is it mainly about people/community outcomes rather than buildings or infrastructure?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student asks for a yes/no fit check on a possible program, which I can provide without writing their submission. I should stay concise, confirm conditionally, and prompt them to justify the fit in their own words to preserve guided discovery and avoid supplying wording.
